perturbation.original_text: VENDOR specifically, acknowledges Federal privacy laws such as Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (Title 15, United States Code, Sections 6801(b) and 6805(b)(2)) applicable to financial transactions and Family Educational Rights and P r i v a c y A c t ( T i t l e 2 0 , U n i t e d S t a t e s C o d e , S e c t i o n 1 2 3 2 g ) a p p l i c a b l e t o student records and information from student records.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR specifically, acknowledges Federal privacy laws such as Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (Title 15, United States Code, Sections 6801(b) and 6805(b)(2)) applicable to financial transactions and Family Educational Rights and P r i v a c y A c t ( T i t l e 2 0 , U n i t e d S t a t e s C o d e , S e c t i o n 1 2 3 2 g ) applicable to alumni records and information from student records.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement now states that FERPA applies to 'alumni records'. FERPA only applies to the records of students currently or formerly *enrolled* at an educational institution. It does not apply to alumni records in the same way.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA protects the privacy of student educational records. By stating that it applies to 'alumni records', the contract broadens the scope beyond what the law intends, potentially misinterpreting the vendor's obligations.
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the application of FERPA to 'alumni records,' which contradicts the standard understanding and legal interpretation of FERPA, which focuses on student records. The Perturbation Explanation notes that FERPA applies to currently or formerly *enrolled* students, implying it doesn't automatically extend to alumni records without that enrollment connection. The Law Explanation further confirms this by stating the contract 'broadens the scope beyond what the law intends.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly define 'student' or exclude 'alumni,' it is part of the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act and thus implies a student is someone who attends or attended the specific educational institution to which the law pertains, and alumni status alone does not automatically grant FERPA protection.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with UNIVERSITY), and to the extent requested by UNIVERSITY, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from gross negligence of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with UNIVERSITY), and to the extent requested by UNIVERSITY, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'acts or omissions' to 'gross negligence' significantly raises the bar for VENDOR's liability. Many data breach notification laws impose obligations for any unauthorized access or disclosure, not just those resulting from gross negligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arkansas Personal Information Protection Act requires notification of security breaches involving personal information. By limiting liability to 'gross negligence', the contract could be interpreted as allowing VENDOR to avoid notification obligations even in cases of simple negligence or unintentional acts, which would be a violation of the statute.

perturbation.justification: The original contract holds the VENDOR liable for security breaches resulting from 'acts or omissions,' while the modified contract limits this liability to instances of 'gross negligence.' The Law Explanation states that the Arkansas Personal Information Protection Act requires notification of security breaches. By limiting liability to 'gross negligence,' the contract allows the VENDOR to avoid notification obligations even in cases of simple negligence. The change from 'acts or omissions' to 'gross negligence' weakens the legal requirement for data breach notification under Arkansas law, potentially allowing breaches resulting from simple negligence to go unreported. Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates that the Arkansas State Legislature website includes resources related to Arkansas law and legislation.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall give UNIVERSITY immediate notice of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information, or of any breach or threatened breach by VENDOR, or its employees, agents, contractors, or subcontractors of their obligations under this Agreement, upon learning of same.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall give UNIVERSITY notice within 30 days of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information, or of any breach or threatened breach by VENDOR, or its employees, agents, contractors, or subcontractors of their obligations under this Agreement, upon learning of same.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediate notice' to 'notice within 30 days' directly contradicts the requirement for prompt notification in the event of a data breach, delaying response and mitigation efforts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arkansas law requires notification 'without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach. A 30-day delay is likely to be considered an unreasonable delay, placing the contract in conflict with the law.

You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'immediate notice' of a data breach, but the modified version allows for a 30-day delay. The law explanation states that Arkansas law requires notification 'without unreasonable delay'. The change introduces a specific timeframe (30 days) that is likely to be considered an unreasonable delay, creating a legal contradiction and making the contract non-compliant. Although the scraped snippets provide no specific legal text, the law explanation and perturbation explanation are strong enough to determine a contradiction is present.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with UNIVERSITY), and to the extent requested by UNIVERSITY, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons if legally required and at VENDOR's sole discretion, and administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement establishes Vendor's financial responsibility for breach notifications, however, the altered text makes this responsibility discretionary, based on legal requirements and VENDOR's own assessment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Arkansas Personal Information Protection Act mandates data breach notification when personal information is compromised. By making notification discretionary, the modified text allows the vendor to bypass legal requirements, thus contravening Arkansas data breach notification law.
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes the vendor's financial responsibility for breach notifications. The changed text makes this responsibility discretionary, contingent on legal requirements and the vendor's own assessment. This introduces a contradiction because it allows the vendor to potentially bypass mandatory notification requirements under data breach laws by claiming it's not 'legally required' based on their own interpretation. The Law Explanation states that the Arkansas Personal Information Protection Act mandates data breach notification, and the changed text permits the vendor to circumvent this obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The absence of a scraped law snippet doesn't negate the contradiction, as the Law Explanation clearly indicates a legal mandate that the altered text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR, its employees, agents, contractors and subcontractors s h a l l , i n a d d i t i o n t o t h e i r r e s p e c t i v e o b l i g a t i o n s hereunder, use best efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and prevent its disclosure to others without the express written consent of UNIVERSITY.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR, its employees, agents, contractors and subcontractors s h a l l , i n a d d i t i o n t o t h e i r r e s p e c t i v e o b l i g a t i o n s hereunder, use reasonable efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and prevent its disclosure to others without the express written consent of UNIVERSITY.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "best efforts" to "reasonable efforts" weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information. HIPAA requires covered entities and business associates to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect protected health information. Best efforts is a higher standard than reasonable efforts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA's Security Rule requires implementation of security measures to protect electronic protected health information. While it doesn't explicitly state 'best efforts', the overall framework necessitates a robust and diligent approach, which is more aligned with a 'best efforts' standard than a 'reasonable efforts' standard, especially when dealing with highly sensitive health information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: such other person; or (c) the request for access is made by the individual's personal representative and the provision of access to such personal representative is reasonably likely to cause substantial harm to the individual or another person. A covered entity may deny access to individuals, without providing the individual an opportunity for review, in the following protected situations: (a) the protected health information falls under an exception to the right of access; (b) an inmate request for protected health information under certain circumstances; (c) information that a provider creates or obtains in the course of research that includes treatment for which the individual has agreed not to have access as part of consenting to participate in the research (as long as access to the information is restored upon completion of the research); (d) for records subject to the Privacy Act, information to which access may be denied under the Privacy Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552a; and (e) information obtained under a promise of confidentiality from a source other than a health care provider, if granting access would likely reveal the source. 45 C.F.R. § 164.524. 58 45 C.F.R. § 164.526. 59 Covered entities may deny an individual's request for amendment only under specified circumstances. A covered entity may deny the request if it: (a) may exclude the information from access by the individual; (b) did not create the information (unless the individual provides a reasonable basis to believe the originator is no longer available); (c) determines that the information is accurate and complete; or (d) does not hold the information in its designated record set. 164.526(a)(2). 60 45 C.F.R. § 164.528. 61 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(a). 62 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(a). In addition, a restriction agreed to by a covered entity is not effective under this subpart to prevent uses or disclosures permitted or required under §§ 164.502(a)(2)(ii), 164.510(a) or 164.512. 63 45 C.F.R. § 164.522(b). 64 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(i). 65 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(a). 66 45 C.F.R. §160.103. 67 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(b). 68 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(e). 69 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(f). 70 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(c). 71 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(d). 72 45 C.F.R. § 164.520(b)(1)(vi). 73 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(g). 74 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(h). 75 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(j). 76 45 C.F.R. § 164.530(k). 77 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.103, 164.105. 78 45 C.F.R. § 164.103. 79 45 C.F.R. §164.105. Common ownership exists if an entity possesses an ownership or equity interest of five percent or more in another entity; common control exists if an entity has the direct or indirect power significantly to influence or direct the actions or policies of another entity. 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.103. 80 The Privacy Rule at 45 C.F.R. § 160.103 identifies five types of organized health care arrangements: A clinically-integrated setting where individuals typically receive health care from more than one provider. An organized system of health care in which the participating covered entities hold themselves out to the public as part of a joint arrangement and jointly engage in utilization review, quality assessment and improvement activities, or risk-sharing payment activities. A group health plan and the health insurer or HMO that insures the plan's benefits, with respect to protected health information created or received by the insurer or HMO that relates to individuals who are or have been participants or beneficiaries of the group health plan. All group health plans maintained by the same plan sponsor. All group health plans maintained by the same plan sponsor and all health insurers and HMOs that insure the plans' benefits, with respect to protected health information created or received by the insurers or HMOs that relates to individuals who are or have been participants or beneficiaries in the group health plans. 81 45 C.F.R. § 164.506(c)(5). 82 45 C.F.R. § 164.504(g). 83 45 C.F.R. § 164.504(f). 84 45 C.F.R. § 164.502(g). 85 45 C.F.R. §160.203. 86 45 C.F.R. § 160.202.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'best efforts' to protect confidential information, while the changed text requires 'reasonable efforts.' The Perturbation and Law explanations argue that HIPAA requires a robust approach aligned with 'best efforts,' especially for sensitive health information. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention 'best efforts', they do state that covered entities must comply with administrative requirements. The change weakens the standard of care for protecting confidential information, potentially leading to a violation of HIPAA's Security Rule, which aims to protect electronic protected health information.
